elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate the concept of algorithmic replicability introduced by \citet{impagliazzo2022reproducibility,ghazi2021user,kwangjun2024 in an online setting.}
elements.sent-2411-002: In our model, the input sequence received by the online learner is generated from time-varying distributions chosen by an adversary (obliviously).
elements.sent-2411-003: Our objective is to design low-regret online algorithms that, with high probability, produce the exact same sequence of actions when run on two independently sampled input sequences generated as described above.
elements.sent-2411-004: We refer to such algorithms as adversarially replicable.
elements.sent-2411-005: Previous works (such as \cite{esfandiari2022replicable ) explored replicability in the online setting under inputs generated independently from a fixed distribution; we term this notion as iid-replicability.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Our model generalizes to capture both adversarial and iid input sequences, as well as their mixtures, which can be modeled by setting certain distributions as point-masses.
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate adversarially replicable online learning algorithms for online linear optimization and the experts problem that achieve sub-linear regret.
elements.sent-2411-008: Additionally, we propose a general framework for converting an online learner into an adversarially replicable one within our setting, bounding the new regret in terms of the original algorithm’s regret.
elements.sent-2411-009: We also present a nearly optimal (in terms of regret) iid-replicable online algorithm for the experts problem, highlighting the distinction between the iid and adversarial notions of replicability.
elements.sent-2411-010: Finally, we establish lower bounds on the regret (in terms of the replicability parameter and time) that any replicable online algorithm must incur.
elements.sent-2411-011: The replicability crisis, which is pervasive across scientific disciplines, has substantial implications for the integrity and reliability of findings.
elements.sent-2411-012: In a survey of 1,500 researchers, it was reported that 70\% had attempted but failed to replicate another researcher’s findings~\citep{baker20161 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: A recent Nature article \citep{ball2023ai discusses how the replicability crisis in AI is creating a ripple effect across numerous scientific fields, including medicine, due to AI’s broad applications.}
elements.sent-2411-014: This highlights an urgent need to establish a formal framework for assessing the replicability of experiments in machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-015: Driven by this context,~\citet{impagliazzo2022reproducibility (see also \cite{ghazi2021user,kwangjun2024 ) initiate the study of reproducibility as a property of algorithms themselves, rather than the process by which their results are collected and reported.}}

elements.sent-2411-020: In this work, we focus on studying and designing replicable online learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-021: In the context of an online learning algorithm, say for instance for the setting of learning with experts advice or online linear optimization, $\rho$-replicability means that when the algorithm is run using the same internal randomness on two input sequences sampled iid from an (unknown) distribution $\calD$, the entire sequence of actions performed is identical in both the runs with probability at least $1-\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-022: More precisely, suppose the inputs to the an online algorithm, say $\ALG$, arrive one by one, i.e., input $c_t$ at time $t$, where $c_t\in \chi$ for some input domain $\chi$.

elements.paragraph-2411-002: Rounding of cumulative cost vectors to the grid points in a random grid
elements.sent-2411-061: Here, at the end of each block, the online learner rounds the cumulative cost vectors to the grid points in a grid with a random offset, and selects the action that minimizes the total cost pretending the grid point is the cost vector.
elements.sent-2411-062: This idea is inspired by the seminal ``Follow the Lazy Leader'' ($\FLL$) algorithm of ~\citet{kalai2005efficient .}

elements.section-2411-005: A General Framework for Replicable Online Learning
elements.sent-2411-259: In this section, we demonstrate a general recipe for converting a regret minimization algorithm $\ALGINT$, which we call the internal algorithm, to an adveresarially $\rho$-replicable algorithm $\ALGEXT$ with potentially a higher regret.
elements.sent-2411-260: Our general procedure uses two main ideas, a rounding procedure of the loss trajectories inspired by the ``Follow the Lazy Leader''($\FLL$) algorithm of~\citet{kalai2005efficient , and blocking of time-steps.}
elements.sent-2411-261: We present results for both setting where the internal algorithm $\ALGINT$ takes as input bounded $\ell_1$ or $\ell_{\infty}$ cost vectors.

elements.sent-2411-262: In this section, we consider the case where the internal algorithm $\ALGINT$ takes as input unit-bounded $\ell_1$ cost vectors.
elements.sent-2411-263: Our framework is as follows: At each timestep $t$ if $t-1$ is a multiple of the block size $B$, then, it picks an $n$-dimensional grid with spacing $1/\eps$ where $\eps$ is given as input, and a random offset $p_t\sim[0,1/\eps)^n$, where $n$ is the dimension of the space.

elements.sent-2411-417: Further, when the coin's bias is either $1/2+\tau$ or $1/2-\tau$, $a= \majority\set{a_t\mid t\in [T]}$ is a good indicator of the coin's bias: we answer that the coin has bias $1/2+\tau$ if $a=2$ and $1/2-\tau$ if $a=1$.
elements.sent-2411-418: Also, we will be correct with probability at least $1-\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-419: To see this, suppose that the coin has bias $1/2+\tau$ and notice that the expected cost of ALG is (the expectations below are over the internal randomness of the algorithm and the coin flips leading to the sequence $c_1,\ldots,c_T$): \begin{equation}\Ex[Cost(ALG)] = \Ex[\sum_{j=1}^T \I[a_t=1]c_t(1) + \I[a_t=2]c_t(2)] = \sum_{j=1}^T\Ex[\I[a_t=1]c_t(1) + \I[a_t=2]c_t(2)] \qquad (Linearity of Expectation) = \sum_{j=1}^T\Ex[\I[a_t=1](1/2+\tau) + \I[a_t=2](1/2-\tau)] \qquad ($\because$ $a_t$ is independent of $c_t$) = T/2 - \tau \Ex\left[\left(\sum_{j=1}^T \I[a_t=2] - \I[a_t=1]\right)\right].\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-078: \begin{equation}\Ex[Cost(ALG)] = \Ex[\sum_{j=1}^T \I[a_t=1]c_t(1) + \I[a_t=2]c_t(2)] = \sum_{j=1}^T\Ex[\I[a_t=1]c_t(1) + \I[a_t=2]c_t(2)] \qquad (Linearity of Expectation) = \sum_{j=1}^T\Ex[\I[a_t=1](1/2+\tau) + \I[a_t=2](1/2-\tau)] \qquad ($\because$ $a_t$ is independent of $c_t$) = T/2 - \tau \Ex\left[\left(\sum_{j=1}^T \I[a_t=2] - \I[a_t=1]\right)\right].\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-420: Using the fact that the regret is at most $K$ and that $\Ex[\sum_{j=1 ^T c_j(2)] = T/2 -\tau T$ (where the expectation is over the coin flips) we get: \begin{equation}T/2 - \tau \Ex\left[\left(\sum_{j=1}^T \I[a_t=2] - \I[a_t=1]\right)\right] \leq T/2 - \tau T +K \implies \Ex\left[\left(\sum_{j=1}^T \I[a_t=2] - \I[a_t=1]\right)\right] \geq T-K/\tau = (1-\delta)T \quad\text{(using $\tau = \frac{K}{\delta T}$)} \implies \Pr[\majority\set{a_t\mid t\in [T]} = 2]\times T \geq (1-\delta)T \implies \Pr[\majority\set{a_t\mid t\in [T]} = 2] > 1-\delta.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-463: Furthermore: \begin{equation}\E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(\ALGINT,\Shat)] = \sum_{u=1}^{T/B} \E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(a_u,g_{uB}-g'_{(u-1)B})]\label{eq:blackbox-eq7-experts} =\sum_{u=1}^{T/B} \E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(a_u,g_{uB})] - \E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(a_u,g'_{(u-1)B})]\label{eq:blackbox-eq10-experts} \intertext{now using the fact that $a_u$ is independent of both $g'_{(u-1)B}$ and $g_{uB}$ we have (note that $a_u$ depends on $g_{(u-1)B}$ and $g'_{(u-2)B}$),} =\sum_{u=1}^{T/B} \Ex_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(a_u,c_{(u-1)B:uB})]\label{eq:blackbox-eq2-experts}\end{equation} Putting together~\Cref{eq:blackbox-eq9-experts,eq:blackbox-eq2-experts implies that: \begin{equation}\E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(\ALGINT,\Shat)]=\Ex_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(\ALGEXT,S)]\label{eq:blackbox-eq3-experts}\end{equation} Now, combining~\Cref{eq:blackbox-eq0-experts,eq:blackbox-eq3-experts implies: \begin{equation}\Ex_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(\ALGEXT, S)]=(B+2/\eps) \E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(\ALGINT,\Stilde)]\label{eq:blackbox-eq6-experts}\end{equation} Now, for any action $a\in \calA$, we bound $\E_{\REXT [\cost(a,\Stilde)]$ as follows: \begin{equation}\E_{\REXT}[\cost(a,\Stilde)] =(1/(B+2/\eps))\E_{\REXT}[\cost(a,\Shat)] \label{eq:blackbox-eq4-experts} =(1/(B+2/\eps))\sum_{u=1}^{T/B} \E_{\REXT}[\cost(a,g_{uB}-g'_{(u-1)B})] = (1/(B+2/\eps)) \sum_{u=1}^{T/B} \cost(a, c_{(u-1)B:uB}) = (1/(B+2/\eps)) \cost(a, c_{1:T}) = (1/(B+2/\eps)) \cost(a, S)\label{eq:blackbox-eq5-experts}\end{equation} Now, combining~\Cref{eq:blackbox-eq02-experts,eq:blackbox-eq6-experts,eq:blackbox-eq5-experts implies that for any action $a\in \calA$: \begin{equation}\frac{\E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(\ALGEXT,S)]}{B+2/\eps} \leq \frac{\cost(a,S)}{B+2/\eps} +\regret_{T/B}(\ALGINT)\end{equation} which implies that for any sequence $S$ and any action $a\in \calA$: \begin{equation}\E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(\ALGEXT,S)] \leq \cost(a,S) +(B+2/\eps)\regret_{T/B}(\ALGINT)\end{equation} and the proof is complete.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-097: \begin{equation}\E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(\ALGINT,\Shat)] = \sum_{u=1}^{T/B} \E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(a_u,g_{uB}-g'_{(u-1)B})]\label{eq:blackbox-eq7-experts} =\sum_{u=1}^{T/B} \E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(a_u,g_{uB})] - \E_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(a_u,g'_{(u-1)B})]\label{eq:blackbox-eq10-experts} \intertext{now using the fact that $a_u$ is independent of both $g'_{(u-1)B}$ and $g_{uB}$ we have (note that $a_u$ depends on $g_{(u-1)B}$ and $g'_{(u-2)B}$),} =\sum_{u=1}^{T/B} \Ex_{\RINT,\REXT}[\cost(a_u,c_{(u-1)B:uB})]\label{eq:blackbox-eq2-experts}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-464: By~\Cref{lem:regret-general-framework-ell-infty , the regret of~\Cref{alg:general-framework-ell-infty is $(B+2/\eps)\regret_{T/B (\ALGINT)$, therefore, in order to minimize the regret, we need to set $B=2/\eps$, which implies that $\rho=2\eps^2 m T=\frac{8mT {B^2}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-465: Consequently, $B=\sqrt{\frac{8mT {\rho }$, and the regret of~\Cref{alg:general-framework-ell-infty is $2B \regret_{T/B (\ALGINT)$.}}}}
